title: Top Five Summer 2021 Movies and Everything Sean Missed While He Was Out
format: Sean is back from parental leave with two months of half-remembered notes on Roadrunner, Black Widow, and Space Jam. Amanda walks him through the discourse, then they compare what was actually worth watching this summer.

segments.heading: Roadrunner and the ethics of AI in documentary

films.title: Roadrunner: A Film About Anthony Bourdain
films.note: Director Morgan Neville used an AI voice model to re-create three lines Bourdain wrote but never recorded, and mentioned it almost offhand in a New Yorker interview. Sean interviewed Neville for the show and didn't know about the AI or the decision to leave Asia Argento out entirely.

segments.heading: Corporate synergy and Space Jam: A New Legacy

films.title: Space Jam: A New Legacy
films.note: Sean watched 20 minutes, called it absolute trash, and mostly softened after hearing Chris Vernon's 'it's a kids' movie, don't overthink it' take on The Mismatch.

segments.heading: Black Widow, Scarlett Johansson, and a lawsuit that reset how deals get made

films.title: Black Widow
films.note: Underperformed relative to other MCU entries but held up fine against the summer's overall box office. The real story is Johansson suing Disney over the simultaneous Disney+ release cutting into her backend.

films.title: Cruella
films.note: Emma Stone quietly settled her own backend dispute with Disney about a week after reports she was considering suing too.

segments.heading: The House of Gucci trailer, and a stacked Thanksgiving weekend

films.title: House of Gucci
films.note: The trailer alone lifted Sean's mood for the whole summer: the accents, Jared Leto's costuming, Lady Gaga playing Italian-American like she's doing Rasputin.

films.title: The Beatles: Get Back
films.note: Peter Jackson's six-hour Fab Four documentary, arriving one day after Gucci. They're counting all six hours as a movie.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: PTA's next film, known here only by its production-era working title, 'Soggy Bottom.'

segments.heading: Stillwater, Amanda Knox, and a strange Matt Damon performance

films.title: Stillwater
films.note: Amanda Knox objected to the film's resemblance to her story after press coverage tagged it the 'Amanda Knox saga.' Sean: absorbing for the first hour and forty-five minutes, a disaster after.

segments.heading: The Suicide Squad bombs in theaters — maybe not on HBO Max

films.title: The Suicide Squad
films.note: 90% on Rotten Tomatoes and reportedly one of the year's biggest theatrical bombs. Sean liked it for lacking the 'we're all friends here' Marvel sheen.

segments.heading: Uh-oh movies: a fall logjam and one very quiet bomb
segments.note: release dates and a $2 million opening weekend

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: Its trailer became the most-watched in movie history, which may be why Venom keeps getting nudged around the release calendar.

films.title: Reminiscence
films.note: An $80 million Hugh Jackman sci-fi film released day-and-date on HBO Max that made $2 million in its opening weekend.

segments.heading: The best movies of the summer

films.title: The Green Knight
films.note: Amanda spent $60 renting it three times after streaming and 4K problems; admired it more than she enjoyed it. Sean calls it a four-star movie, the best-made thing of the summer.

films.title: The Night House
films.note: Sean watched it on an iPad in a hospital recliner within 24 hours of his daughter's birth. Rebecca Hall carries a genuinely upsetting film.

films.title: Pig
films.note: Nicolas Cage as a reclusive former chef hunting down whoever stole his truffle pig, not the revenge thriller the premise implies. Sean calls it one of Cage's best performances in years.

films.title: CODA
films.note: Amanda disliked it out of Sundance, then rewatched it after having a baby and cried. Formulaic and manipulative, and it worked on her anyway.

films.title: Annette
films.note: A Sparks musical starring Adam Driver and Marion Cotillard, directed by Leos Carax. Sean admires the ambition and can't get past the songs.

films.title: Lily Topples the World
films.note: Domino-art documentary that won the SXSW jury prize Sean sat on this year.

films.title: Val
films.note: Val Kilmer's self-shot archival documentary about his own career, assembled after losing his voice to throat cancer. Moving, but noticeably incurious about his on-set reputation.

films.title: Zola
films.note: Amanda's late add: adapted from the viral Twitter thread, and the movie she's heard the most about from people who don't otherwise follow movies.

films.title: Free Guy
films.note: Sean's most animated take of the episode: cynical and recycled, undercut by its own third-act cameo dump of Marvel and Star Wars props.

films.title: Jungle Cruise
films.note: A kids'-movie version of The Lost City of Z, per Amanda, buried under CGI despite an action director.

films.title: Respect
films.note: A formulaic, two-and-a-half-hour Aretha Franklin biopic. The studio-session scenes land; Jennifer Hudson's rendition of the title song doesn't clear the bar Franklin set.

films.title: Old
films.note: M. Night Shyamalan's beach-trap thriller: strong for the first 100 minutes, undone for both hosts by an ending that isn't really a twist.

films.title: Candyman
films.note: Nia DaCosta's continuation, produced and co-written by Jordan Peele. Sean: every character explains the movie's themes out loud, which makes it play more like an essay than a horror film.

films: King Arthur: Legend of the Sword
